script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Monday — here is your QQQM recap. The portfolio closed one point five nine percent down on the session. It was a broad-based selloff concentrated in semiconductors and high-beta technology names, with a handful of defensive and large-cap software positions offering partial shelter.

A global memory component crisis is reshaping the semiconductor landscape in real time. Micron closed down five point five one percent on Monday, and the pressure extends well beyond one stock — second-quarter global smartphone shipments just fell to their lowest level since twenty thirteen, driven directly by a shortage of key memory components. That data point, reported by Counterpoint Research, puts the entire chip supply chain on watch.

Markets traded lower on Monday as a new Hormuz blockade sent oil prices sharply higher, rattling risk assets across the board. The energy shock reintroduced macro uncertainty at precisely the wrong moment — ahead of a heavy week of technology earnings. Chip stocks bore the brunt of the selling, while broader indices retreated, with the Nasdaq declining on the session as investors rotated defensively and trimmed exposure to high-multiple growth names.

Micron Technology was the headline story on Monday, and the numbers demand attention. The stock closed at nine hundred thirty-seven dollars, down fifty-four dollars and sixty-four cents — a loss of five point five one percent on the session. The catalyst is structural, not just sentiment-driven. Counterpoint Research's latest data shows global smartphone shipments in the second quarter fell to a thirteen-year low, and memory component availability is cited as the primary constraint. That directly pressures Micron's near-term revenue visibility, given the company's significant exposure to mobile DRAM and NAND flash markets. Adding to the complexity, Jim Cramer noted Monday that South Korean investors are aggressively repricing SK Hynix following a sharp post-listing correction in Seoul, even as he argued Korean markets are underestimating the durability of AI memory demand. That divergence between consumer memory weakness and AI memory strength is the fault line Micron now sits on. The position is five point seven five percent of this portfolio, making it a material driver of Monday's drawdown. The critical watch item is Micron's next earnings print and any forward guidance on HBM allocation toward hyperscaler customers — that is the data point that will determine whether Monday's selloff was a buying opportunity or an early warning.

Nvidia closed at two hundred three dollars and fifty-three cents, up zero point three seven percent on Monday. The gain was modest but notable given the pressure on the broader semiconductor complex. Commentary from the Ollama chief executive on Yahoo Finance highlighted growing Fortune five hundred adoption of open-source AI models — a trend that does not diminish Nvidia's infrastructure role but does introduce longer-term questions about compute intensity per workload. Cramer's commentary on SK Hynix and AI memory demand is indirectly constructive for Nvidia's HBM partnerships. Watch for any hyperscaler capital expenditure guidance this earnings cycle.

Apple closed at three hundred seventeen dollars and thirty-one cents, up zero point three four percent. A Counterpoint report confirmed Apple iPhone shipments outperformed the broader smartphone market in the second quarter, even as the global market hit a thirteen-year low. That relative strength is meaningful given the macro pressure. Watch iPhone unit data as second-quarter earnings approach.

Alphabet closed at three hundred fifty-two dollars and fifty-one cents, down one point seven eight percent. Seeking Alpha's second-quarter preview highlighted a four hundred sixty-two billion dollar cloud backlog alongside surging capital expenditure commitments. Separately, Alphabet is moving to commercialize its Tensor Processing Units by selling compute capacity to external cloud providers and launching a neocloud rental offering — a direct push into territory currently dominated by Nvidia. The earnings print is the near-term catalyst.

Microsoft closed at three hundred ninety dollars and ninety-nine cents, up one point seven two percent, making it one of Monday's clearest bright spots in the portfolio. The gain came against a weak tape, suggesting investors are positioning constructively ahead of earnings. Azure growth figures will be the number to watch when the company reports.

Amazon closed at two hundred forty-seven dollars and thirty-one cents, up a marginal zero point one one percent. The stock essentially went nowhere on Monday, which in the context of a broad selloff represents quiet relative resilience. AWS cloud commentary during earnings season will be the dominant catalyst.

Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred thirty-four dollars and thirty-nine cents, down two point two six percent. The stock was caught in the same semiconductor downdraft that hit the broader chip complex on Monday. No company-specific news drove the move. Watch for any data center GPU commentary from peers ahead of Advanced Micro Devices' own earnings update.

Tesla closed at three hundred ninety-four dollars and seventy-six cents, down two point nine zero percent. A piece circulating Monday argued that Tesla's recent price stagnation mirrors historical consolidation patterns that have preceded sharp recoveries. That framing is speculative, but the stock's sensitivity to macro risk-off sentiment was visible in Monday's move. Robotaxi regulatory developments remain the medium-term catalyst.

Intel closed at one hundred three dollars and twelve cents, down eight point three seven percent — the steepest percentage loss among primary holdings on Monday. The move was severe and the stock is the smallest primary holding at two point nine zero percent of the portfolio, but an eight-plus percent single-session decline warrants attention. No company-specific news was tagged to Intel in Monday's flow, suggesting the selloff reflects broader semiconductor sector pressure and possibly positioning ahead of earnings. The stock's recovery trajectory from its prolonged restructuring remains fragile. Any foundry partnership news or earnings guidance update will be closely watched.

Three data points to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, Alphabet reports its second-quarter earnings this week — the cloud backlog figure, Tensor Processing Unit commercialization traction, and advertising revenue growth will each have direct read-through to the six point one eight percent position in this portfolio. Second, any Federal Reserve speaker commentary on the rate path following Monday's oil price spike from the Hormuz blockade could reprice risk assets broadly — elevated energy inflation complicates the easing timeline. Third, Microsoft's second-quarter earnings are approaching — Azure growth and artificial intelligence monetization disclosures will be the critical metrics for the four point five two percent position here and for sentiment across the large-cap technology complex.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Keep close watch on the semiconductor complex — the divergence between AI memory demand and consumer memory weakness is the defining tension heading into this earnings cycle, and it will not resolve quietly.
